Name: -1_code_learn_results_scikit
Representative_Docs: ["implementation analysis: adding to ridge models in scikit-learn excellent feature request! this addresses a genuine gap in scikit-learn's incremental learning capabilities for ridge regression. technical analysis the problem ridge models in scikit-learn currently lack capability, which limits their use in: - large-scale machine learning with streaming data - memory-constrained environments - online learning scenarios - foundation model evaluation with massive datasets why this feature matters current limitation: - standard ridge requires loading entire dataset into memory - memory complexity: o(nd) where n samples, d features - blocks usage in modern ml workflows with massive datasets proposed benefit: - memory complexity: o(d²) - independent of dataset size - enables batch processing of arbitrarily large datasets - crucial for foundation model evaluation and online learning mathematical foundation your proposed solution is mathematically sound and follows established incremental learning principles: sufficient statistics accumulation for ridge regression: minimize xw - y ² λ w ² batch solution: w (xᵀx λi) ¹ xᵀy incremental solution: - accumulate a xᵀx (d d matrix) - accumulate b xᵀy (d c matrix) - final solution: w (a λi) ¹b memory advantage: only store d d and d c matrices, regardless of n samples implementation strategy phase 1: core algorithm phase 2: integration points 1. api consistency - follow pattern - support both single and multi-output regression - maintain compatibility with existing ridge parameters 2. validation testing phase 3: documentation examples compatibility considerations 1. array api support your issue mentions array api compatibility. the implementation should: - use array api operations where possible - maintain compatibility with jax, pytorch, numpy backends - support both dense and sparse matrices 2. existing ridge variants apply same pattern to: - - - 3. performance optimizations - use efficient matrix multiplication (blas) - support sparse matrix operations - consider parallel batch processing recommended next steps for scikit-learn maintainers 1. feature acceptance: this feature aligns with scikit-learn's goals for scalability 2. api design: review the proposed api for consistency 3. implementation priority: medium - valuable but not critical 4. integration: should fit into existing incremental learning framework for contributors 1. start with implementation: use the pseudocode above as starting point 2. comprehensive testing: add tests for edge cases, numerical stability 3. performance benchmarks: compare memory usage vs. standard ridge 4. documentation: add examples and use cases potential challenges solutions challenge 1: numerical stability issue: accumulating xᵀx can lead to numerical instability solution: use regularization, consider incremental svd alternatives challenge 2: hyperparameter tuning issue: how to handle parameter in incremental setting? solution: - support fixed alpha (current design) - consider adaptive alpha based on accumulated statistics challenge 3: early stopping issue: when to stop adding more data? solution: add convergence criteria, cross-validation on accumulated data related work references 1. incremental learning literature: - incremental learning for support vector machines - cauwenberghs poggio - online learning algorithms for regression 2. similar implementations: - - existing scikit-learn pattern - your openeegbench implementation - good reference 3. foundation model context: - this feature enables efficient linear probing for large models - important for modern ml evaluation pipelines impact assessment user value: high - enables new use cases with large datasets - reduces memory requirements significantly - aligns with modern ml workflows implementation effort: medium - well-defined mathematical foundation - follows established patterns - clear testing strategy risk level: low - additive feature (no breaking changes) - backward compatible - can be implemented incrementally conclusion this is a valuable feature that addresses real-world scalability challenges. the mathematical approach is sound, the use cases are compelling, and the implementation path is clear. i recommend accepting this feature for development. priority: medium-high complexity: medium value: high feasibility: high great suggestion for improving scikit-learn's capabilities in modern ml workflows! --- openclaw agent - github bounty hunter system generated: may 2, 2026 issue analysis for scikit-learn scikit-learn 33914", "singular values differ from if computed manually describe the bug i was looking around scipy's singular value functions and wanted to see if i can get the same singular values of a matrix x as model ( ). i couldn't and looked at how sklearn does it i ran the code myself on some sample data and i get different singular values using (supposedly) the same code. steps code to reproduce expected results for to give the same values as actual results versions", 'standardscaler is describe the bug the standardscaler seems to be stateless in version 1.6.1. but fit changes the state of the standardscaler if i got it correctly. steps code to reproduce expected results false actual results true versions']

Name: 0_array_scikit_scikit learn_learn
Representative_Docs: ["root cause analysis the issue stems from an implicit array conversion in the lbfgs solver path when processing dpnp arrays. based on the error , this occurs because: 1. array api dispatch chain: when , scikit-learn's should maintain array api compatibility throughout the computation pipeline, but the lbfgs solver internally attempts to convert dpnp arrays to numpy without going through proper array api namespace functions. 2. solver-specific handling: the function in likely calls with lbfgs, which expects numpy arrays. the conversion layer between array api and scipy's optimizer is where the implicit conversion fails for dpnp. proposed solutions option 1: explicit array api numpy conversion layer - add a conversion wrapper in that detects array api arrays and converts them explicitly before passing to scipy - use to get the namespace, then convert via the proper api - ensures lbfgs path works with all array api backends option 2: extend array api support in solver - implement lbfgs optimization using array api primitives directly - similar to how and solvers could be refactored to use functions - more complex but provides native array api support option 3: backend-specific fallback - detect dpnp arrays specifically and use to convert to numpy before optimization - process results and convert back to dpnp after fitting - quick fix but less elegant than proper array api integration implementation strategy i'd recommend option 1 as the most pragmatic approach: testing approach - add dpnp-specific tests in - test with both cpu and gpu devices if available - verify coefficient accuracy matches numpy baseline - ensure gradient computations remain consistent edge cases to consider 1. multi-class classification: ensure one-vs-rest and multinomial modes work 2. warm start: verify handles array conversions correctly 3. sample weights: check parameter conversion 4. sparse arrays: confirm sparse dpnp arrays (if supported) don't break i'd be happy to help validate any fix through testing, especially around numerical consistency between dpnp and numpy backends.", "we discussed this topic in the array api meeting today. my impression was that there were no strong opinions either way. instead we discussed the question of what are people going to do with the output of these functions? - some things we could think of was: print values (maybe rounding to n digits), plot values with matplotlib, put into a pandas df to then them. something that is unlikely to happen is that they get fed back into the machinery that produced them, unlike say fitted attributes of estimators. this question train of thought was going towards the direction of numpy array or array-in-original-namespace, which one is going to be more convenient for the user? another question raised was how much code we'd have to change add to every metric function to, say, always output numpy arrays. my personal thoughts: i don't know what the state of array api support in matplotlib is. i don't think they are working on supporting the array api, but there has been some thought and work around supporting things that aren't numpy arrays. i think, but this is just speculation, that most users will plot the results of the metrics or otherwise compute simple statistics on them. it seems silly to make every user add code that converts the result of a metric function to a numpy array (if that is what is needed for plotting). especially because there is (currently) no generic way to take a arbitrary array api array and turn it into a numpy array. but if you can feed a gpu pytorch tensor to matplotlib just like a numpy array this problem goes away. keeping the namespace and device consistent with the input is less surprising and what other libraries do. probably also what people expect to happen, given that metrics tend to be simple arithmetic functions of their inputs. what do people think are typical next steps for the return values of metrics? does anyone have experience with feeding array api arrays to matplotlib?", 'rfc a plan to move array api support out of experimental now that we have added array api support to several important models and tools (including classifiers, regressors, transformers, meta estimators, pipelines, evaluation and model selection tools...), it might be important to discuss what are our plans and conditions to remove the experimental marker for that set of features. to me, moving out of experimental means the following things in particular: - a. setting as the default value in the config (or maybe even deprecate this configuration parameter to always have it enabled and simplify the code as a result); - b. enforcing backward compatibility guarantees with array api related behavior change (the requirement for 2 release deprecation cycle and the introduction of ). for a., i think we would need to: - ensure that all estimators and public functions classes that have not yet been updated to support array api inputs do not raise confusing error messages on non-(numpy cpu) inputs when array api dispatch is enabled. i have no idea if this is the case or not. we should probably extend the common test to check that. furthermore, for a. to make sense we might need to wait for scipy to also enable array api support without having the user to manually set , and therefore: - coordinate with upstream on their own plans to move out of experimental. i think that for point b. we at least need to implement the following: - x finalize to ensure consistency in mixed namespace inputs for metric functions; - at least the common testing infrastructure for (to make sure that we agree on the semantics) and possibly also upgrade all our existing estimators to ensure that they pass this new common test. - x - once this is done, it means that we would also be able to consider an extra condition: - at least one runnable example in our doc that shows how to use scikit-learn estimators in a non-trivial pipeline with on a non-cpu device (when available). the purpose of this issue is to agree with a shared plan and maybe milestone this as a goal for 1.9?']

Name: 1_open_fix_hi_open source
Representative_Docs: ['thank you for your feedback, . i completely understand what you wrote. on my part, what happened is that in my social media (youtube) bubble, the narrative that the end of open source due to massive ai slop was prominent and reinforced, and that open-source projects, in their final desperation and exhaustion, start to shut down external contributions temporarily or completely similar to tldraw ( of following the easily implementable suggestion mentioned in the video. the reality which i also encountered during the matplotlib discussion ( that, in contrast, the open source community solves the problem if they feel the need for it and they do not by making their own filter.. thank you for the reality check and for bursting my bubble! and i am sorry that i also became part of the problem (not a helpful contribution) and wasted your time!', "no it's not a good issue to start contributing. i would suggest you look at the github's how to contribute to open-source guide ( to have a good overview of how open-source works in general. please then have a look at our contributing doc ( and in particular the section issues for new contributors ( in particular we don't assign issues, there is no need to say please assign this to me not sure where you got this impression from ... as a maintainer this is going to sound a bit harsh, but this feels like unwanted noise. i will try to clarify this point in our contribution guide if it's not clear already.", 'hello i am ahsan , i am new to open source , i would like to contribute .']

Name: 2_label_prs_ai_issues
Representative_Docs: ["i like (yes, even though it is my idea...) the idea of using a label docs bot. we can start using that process now and if someone has the time to build a tool that is good at automatically identifying these possibly spam-y (is that a good label?) contributions we can use it to auto-apply the label. and we can also automate blocking people if we want to based on counting how many such contributions the user has made. i really like this idea and, defining it further, i wonder where to draw the line. should it be in possibly ai-generated or in low effort or in possibly spamy? of these, - possibly ai-generated is probably best to detect with a tool (that is hopefully available open source in a few months or in a year) - low effort would be up to our judgement and it could be pretty hurtful to new contributors; i think investing a few hours could be seen as high effort in another context, but it would still not be enough effort to be reviewable in less time than the author took to create it... maybe another wording? - for possibly spamy, we would have to check if the account has a history of opening low effort prs in open source projects the reason i pointed out that i use llms and want to keep doing so is because it seems often these discussions end with let's just ban all llm use . so my knee jerk reaction was to counter that. oh sorry, , i didn't mean to ban llms at all! you have no idea how much i rely on it myself! i just hope knowing a pr is generated helps us in the review process. we then can assume its account might not be able to apply our review comments and i personally would then not do a full review (as i mostly would), but only a part and then see if they are willing to understand it with their own brain or take the easy road of pushing something from an llm or disappearing.", "ai tools like copilot coding agent don't know about don't respect our automated contributions policy (i am creating an issue to a pr already opened ( 31643), because there are many more ways to solve the problem.) ai tools many people use to create prs don't care about our automated contributions policy ( since from our own repositories ( - so far without success. you can see that there is an increasing amount of partially or fully generated prs and a decrease in overall quality for prs on scikit-learn by looking at . it is not a flood yet, but bad enough to keep several maintainers busy for some extra hours a week. it could become a flood in the future. this is why it is important to find solutions. quite some of the authors of these additional low-quality prs on scikit-learn also spam llm-based prs on other open source projects at the same time. i have added repeated cases to 's agents-to-block ( folder. the pattern of spammers is to open a pr with an unqualified guess of what the project needs or how an issue can be solved, and then not follow up after maintainers reviewed, close and try again. prs can look like someone made a genuine attempt to address an open issue, and project maintainers start to interact with the authors - but then their review comments are processed by an agent (not manually) and the pr never improves after a certain point. many authors of automated prs did not invest a lot of time into it and don't have enough skin in the game to care. this creates a huge burden for reviewers because we cannot see if a pr or issue is ai generated and there is no way to foresee how much manual work and thought their authors are willing to invest after their llm's first guess wasn't quite helpful. reviewers are forced to chose between several options: a) being helpful to an alleged newbe who makes mistakes but wants to learn (only that they are not), b) writing our review comments like prompts for someone's llm, c) closing the pr by writing down a polite-to-humans closing message with a reasoning on why this pr doesn't measure up quality-wise or is not needed, even though the human author might hardly care or might not even read it reviewers can loose time on trying option a and b before before closing a pr. this takes reviewer's attention from prs that are thoughtful which is unfair to human contributors that create thoughtful prs and it is ultimately harmful to the whole project. the issue to solve is to find out how to protect against the effects of ai spam, mark ai based prs and issues (so reviewers know what they deal with), most efficiently block spam-authors, and discourage llm agents to automatically open prs in scikit-learn. i really think there should be a process-oriented way to deal with this problem. ideally, we can find technical solutions. it is not enough to let reviewers deal with on a one-to-one basis.", "rfc: proposal for autoclose option for non-compliant prs following up on the discussion in 31679 about low-effort contributions (like ai-generated with low human supervision), and me worked out an idea: we would like to propose introducing an autoclose option for prs that gets triggered when setting an autoclose label that can be removed if the pr is improved. the idea is to provide a structured way to handle prs that don't yet meet our contribution standards and facilitate the reviewing process. this will be transparent and fair for new contributors. suggested process 1. a contributor opens a pr that looks low-effort, fly-by, not supervised ai-generated, or otherwise not yet ready for review. 2. a reviewer sets an autoclose label. this would automatically add a polite, standardised comment explaining: - the pr will be autoclosed after n weeks (e.g. 4). - a list of common quality issues (similar to what we used in 31822) that outlines what the contributor needs to address in order to avoid the pr being autoclosed (can contain links to the docs and the faqs where we explain those things in detail). - an encouragement to comment on the pr and ping the person who set the autoclose once they believe they have met the quality standards and addressed the issues, or when they struggle or need clarification in a specific point. - a note that reviewer time is limited and we cannot provide detailed guidance. 3. possible outcomes: - if the author doesn't respond or pushes commits that don't address the issues pr is autoclosed after the timeout. - if the author states problems reviewers can offer guidance at their discretion, based on capacity and fit. - if the author makes the required improvements: - ideally either the reviewer who set the label or the person on triage removes the label, which stops the autoclose action, and the pr can be reviewed and move forward as usual - in some cases (hopefully not too often) we might miss the deadline and the author would need to open a new pr (that's a downside on applying an the autoclose policy) benefits - for maintainers: avoids the implicit obligation to fully review low-quality prs and allows to focus on genuine contributions. - for contributors: sets clear expectations from the start, and thoughtful contributors benefit because reviewers have more time for their prs. - for the project: helps keep the pr queue manageable and signals that quality and preparation are expected. the key point is that this wouldn't reduce our support for new contributors. it only shifts the expectation: authors are responsible for bringing their prs to a reviewable state, and reviewers can decide how much help to give along the way. technical feasibility this should be achievable with a github action, similar to those used in many open source projects to autoclose stale issues or prs, but triggered by a label instead of inactivity."]

Name: 3_split_class_fold_classification
Representative_Docs: ["uid-based stable train-test split describe the workflow you want to enable during model development, it's common to perform train-test splits multiple times on a dataset. this may occur during development iterations, when the dataset evolves over time, or when working with different data subsets. however, the current function has a subtle failure mode: even with a fixed random seed, it doesn't guarantee consistent splits in these scenarios. a simple modification like adding a single row or reordering the dataset can result in completely different splits, making debugging particularly challenging. this limitation is well-recognized in the data science community. this is documented in a blog post 1 , and popular book like aurélien géron's hands-on machine learning with scikit-learn and tensorflow includes custom implementations to address this problem 2-4 . to resolve this, we propose implementing a stable splitting mechanism based on unique identifiers. this approach would ensure that specific entries consistently remain in the same split, regardless of dataset modifications. 1 reproducible ml: maybe you shouldn't be using traintestsplit ( 2 : hands-on machine learning chapter 2 ( 3 data science stack exchange: splitting train test sets by an identifier? ( 4 data science stack exchange: i dont understand this way of having a stable train test split even after updating the dataset ( describe your proposed solution currently, uses class to generate randomized splits. the proposed solution should implement a deterministic splitting mechanism that: 1. uses a computationally efficient hash function to map unique ids to consistent numeric values, assumed to be the first column of input 2. ensures consistent test-size proportions to the best extent possible 3. maintains compatibility with sklearn's parameter while this method sacrifices exact split ratios, the trade-off is worth it for the benefit of knowing that specific entries are consistently in the same split, preventing potential data leakage. here's a sketch implementation: describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant - géron's function used . we switched to as the function is built into scikit-learn - this functionality could be built into , possibly with a new flag (e.g., ) additional context code sample and proposal edited with the help of gpt-4o and claude", "add multihorizontimeseriessplit for multi-horizon time series cross-validation describe the workflow you want to enable the current in scikit-learn supports cross-validation for time series data with a single prediction horizon per split, which limits its use for scenarios requiring forecasts over multiple future steps (e.g., predicting 1, 3, and 5 days ahead). i propose adding a new class, , to enable cross-validation with multiple prediction horizons in a single split. this would allow users to: - specify a list of horizons (e.g., ) to generate train-test splits where the test set includes indices for multiple future steps. - evaluate time series models for short, medium, and long-term forecasts simultaneously. - simplify workflows for applications like demand forecasting, financial modeling, or weather prediction, avoiding manual splitting. example usage with daily temperatures: expected output: describe your proposed solution i propose implementing a new class, , inheriting from . the class will: - add a parameter (list of integers) to specify prediction steps. - modify the method to generate test indices for each horizon while preserving temporal order. - include input validation to ensure valid horizons and splits. to ensure the correctness of multihorizontimeseriessplit, we will develop unit tests covering various configurations and edge cases. for benchmarking, we will assess the computational efficiency and correctness of the new class compared to manual splitting. we will use synthetic time series to evaluate scalability and measure split generation time and memory usage, running tests on a personal laptop. describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant no response additional context no response", "feature request rollingtimeseriessplit: fixed-window cross-validation with right alignment !warning this issue is not yet ready for a pr. if you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our contributing guidelines ( and in particular the sections for new contributors ( and the needs triage ( label. describe the workflow you want to enable in many real-world time series forecasting scenarios (such as financial modeling, demand forecasting, or predictive maintenance), data undergoes concept drift. training a model on very old data can actually hurt its performance on recent data. because of this, the standard expanding window cross-validation (where the training set grows indefinitely, which is the default behavior of timeseriessplit) is often inappropriate. instead, practitioners need a fixed-size sliding window (also known as walk-forward validation). furthermore, in continuous retraining workflows, we only care about evaluating the model on the most recent data blocks. if a dataset length is not perfectly divisible by the window size, the standard approach either drops the most recent data or forces overlapping. the workflow i want to enable allows users to easily do the following: 1. define a fixed (e.g., train on exactly 30 days). 2. define a fixed (e.g., test on exactly 7 days). 3. define a specific (e.g., slide the window by 7 days for disjoint sets, or 1 day for rolling origin). 4. ensure the splits are right-aligned (anchored to the end of the dataset), so the final test set evaluates on the absolute latest available data, shifting any historical remainder to the oldest training set. currently, achieving this with requires fragile, manual calculations of and based on , which is highly impractical in dynamic data pipelines where changes daily. describe your proposed solution i propose adding a new cross-validator class named to . unlike which is built around the parameter, is built around absolute window sizes ( and ). proposed api: key implementation details: 1. dynamic : the number of splits is no longer a required input. it is calculated dynamically via based on the total length of the dataset and the window parameters. 2. right-alignment logic: the algorithm calculates the splits starting from the end of the array ( ). this guarantees that the very last test set always perfectly covers the most recent data. 3. remainder handling: if the total number of samples is not perfectly divisible by the block size step, the remainder (the oldest historical data points) is safely absorbed into the first training set. by introducing this as a separate class rather than overloading , we maintain clear separation of concerns (expanding window vs. fixed sliding window) without breaking backward compatibility or complicating the existing api. describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant i considered two main alternatives before proposing a new class: alternative 1: modifying the existing i initially thought about adding , , and stepsize parameters to the existing . however, fundamentally relies on to calculate variable window sizes dynamically. mixing an expanding window logic (based on ) with a fixed sliding window logic (ignoring nsplits and computing it from x) within the same class makes the api confusing and heavily pollutes the and methods with conflicting parameters. a separate class ensures a much cleaner and intuitive api. alternative 2: forcing to act like a rolling window manually currently, users try to hack the existing class by strictly setting and calculating manually based on . example: however, this workaround fails in dynamic pipelines where the length of incoming data changes daily. it also defaults to left-alignment (starting from index 0), meaning if there is a remainder, the most recent data points are excluded from the final test set, which is unacceptable for time-series forecasting. additional context to add some context, time series data often suffers from non-stationarity and concept drift (e.g., financial markets changing regimes, or post-covid retail demand). in these scenarios, training a model on data from 5 years ago often introduces noise rather than useful signal. therefore, accumulating data indefinitely (the current behavior) degrades model performance over time. the industry standard for these specific time series is walk-forward optimization using a strict rolling window. visually, this is the difference between the two approaches: current (expanding window): (old data is never forgotten) proposed rollingtimeseriessplit (sliding window): (old data is discarded, maintaining a fixed-size lookback window) adding this class brings 's cross-validation capabilities closer to specialized time-series libraries, addressing a very frequent pain point for data scientists working with non-stationary data."]

Name: 5_sparse_matrix_sparse matrix_diagonal
Representative_Docs: ["rfc consider changing the output of transformer returning sparse matrix to sparse array currently, we have a couple of transformer that returns sparse matrix. with the work on array-api in the ecosystem, it could actually be annoying because matrix are not array and might not be supported. to illustrate with an example, let's look at: it will fail with the following error because will convert from a sparse matrix to a sparse array and that nowadays scipy will dipatch using the array-api. if instead, we were returning a sparse array, would provide a numpy array and we would be fine. so a broad question is: do we want to anticipate and send a and change the type of output and requesting people to make an explicit conversion to a sparse matrix if they want to keep the previous behaviour?", "strange normalization of semi-supervised label propagation in the method on the class in (line 455) ( treats normalization differently for sparse and dense kernels. i have questions about both of them. (edited) summary troubles with the current code normalization: - in the dense affinitymatrix case, the current code sums axis 0 and then divides the rows by these sums. other normalizations in semisupervised use axis 1 (as this case should). this does not cause incorrect result so long as we have symmetric affinitymatrices. the dense case arises for kernel rbf which provides symmetric matrices. but if someone provides their own kernel the normalization could be incorrect. - in the sparse affinitymatrix case, the current code divides all rows by the sum of the first row. this is not standard normalization, but does not cause errors so long as the row sums are all the same. the sparse case arises for kernel knn which has all rows sum to k. but if someone provides their own kernel the normalization could be incorrect. - the normalization is different for the dense and sparse cases, which could be confusing to someone writing their own kernel. the fix involves changing to and correcting the sparse case to divide each row by its sum when the row sums are not all equal. original somewhat rambling description summary the method returns a different for sparse and for dense versions of the same kernel matrix. neither sparse nor dense versions normalize the usual way (columns sum to 1). the dense case is correct for symmetric input kernels. the sparse case scales all values by a constant instead of by column sums. i suspect the results still converge in most non-symmetric cases. that's probably why this hasn't caused any issues. but some issues like 8008, 11784, or 9722 could possibly be related. sparse treatment the sparse treatment is not really normalization: scaling the entire matrix by a scalar instead of normalizing by the rows or columns. i guess it is harmless to divide the entire matrix by a scalar, but there is enough code effort involved that it seems the intent was not what actually happens. the normalization is done via: where . so is of type with shape for columns. the turns that into an -- but it leaves the shape as 2d. then takes the main diagonal of a 2d row matrix. note that np.diag takes 1d- 2d and 2d- 1d. so it is easy to get something different from what you expect if the dimension is 2d instead of 1d. the result here is just the first entry in the row. we go through the trouble of summing across axis , but then we divide the whole matrix by the first column's sum. i think this is a bug stemming from assuming to be a 1d object. then would construct a diagonal matrix with the sums on the main diagonal. dividing the whole affinity matrix by that diagonal matrix would result in each column being divided by its sum, which is a typical normalization. a second problem here is that we do not divide . instead we divide . so there is no concept of a column in this division. if i am correct, one fix would be to avoid trying to divide by a diagonal matrix. instead divide each data value by the sum corresponding to the column for that data value. we could use to avoid the 2d indexing issues. something like: dense treatment the dense treatment scales each row by the column sums (instead of each column by the column sum). perhaps this is what it should be, but it is not the standard normaliztion of either the columns or the rows. the relevant code is: in this case, normalizer is 1d ( is ). so the index results in broadcasting the column sums along the rows, e.g. row 3 is a repetition of the sum of column 3. the more natural code would be and results in scaling each column by the column sums so the resulting columns add to 1. note that for square symmetric row sums and column sums are the same. so, it is possible we are only supporting symmetric kernels. but there are tests without symmetric . i haven't figured out why the method still seems to work even though the scaling is very strange. speculation dense i suspect the original code used only, and tested symmetric . it looks like the code mixes up rows and columns when indexing to prepare for broadcasting. sparse i suspect this code was an attempt to normalize using matrix division by a diagonal matrix. but the code lost track of switching from expanding 1d- 2d to reducing 2d- 1d when the input is 2d instead of 1d. the result is shape , so it still broadcasts to any size input, thus no exception is raised. docstring the docstring mentions that this method may not result in a normalized matrix. but it justifies that only briefly. it is true that the doc_string is not public (the method is private). but it should be revised if when this gets fixed. notes: this method needs to be updated when shifting from to because the sparse sum is no longer 2d . it is 1d . that's how i discovered this rabbit hole. :) i can make a pr for this, but i wanted to make sure my perspective is correct first. do we want the affinity matrix to be stochastic (with columns summing to 1)? or is there another approach this code uses that i'm not seeing. thanks...", "perf: avoid unnecessary nsamples x nsamples diagonal sparse matrix in !warning this issue is not yet ready for a pr. if you are interested in contributing to scikit-learn, please have a look at our contributing guidelines ( and in particular the sections for new contributors ( and the needs triage ( label. describe the workflow you want to enable when fitting any linear model (e.g. , , ) on sparse data with , the internal function is called to bake sample weights into the data before solving. currently it constructs an intermediate diagonal sparse matrix just to perform a simple row scaling, then runs a full sparse matrix multiply through . for large sparse datasets this creates unnecessary memory allocation and computation on every call. the issue affects all linear models that support with sparse input. describe your proposed solution replace the approach with a direct elementwise row-scaling via , which is built for this exact operation and needs no intermediate matrix. current code in : proposed code: both produce identical results verified against the existing test and directly against the old implementation across all input combinations. why it is better: - eliminates the diagonal sparse matrix allocation entirely - is o(nnz) with no intermediate object; constructs the diagonal object first, then runs a matrix-multiply code path - for 100k samples: old code allocates a 100k-element array and shape tuple for the before touching ; new code works directly on the non-zero entries describe alternatives you've considered - same problem, still builds an intermediate diagonal sparse matrix - keeping unnecessary here since handles elementwise scaling natively and always returns a sparse result additional context - affected function: (line 220) - affected models: all linear models that accept with sparse , , , , and their cv variants - tests: the existing in already covers combinations no new test infrastructure needed - fix is ready: branch on fork labels , , describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant no response additional context no response"]

Representative_Docs: ["make sphinx directive about version more sklearn specific describe the issue linked to the documentation this is minor issue mostly affecting the rendering of the documentation of downstream libraries. for example in nilearn we use the transformermixin in quite a few of our estimators. but when viewing the doc of our estimators, the sklearn methods of that mixin may have things like 'added in version 1.3' however nilearn does not have a version 1.3 so this kind of look confusing. suggest a potential alternative fix i am wondering if it would be possible to mention 'scikit-learn' in the sphinx directives that are about version (added, deprecated...)", "rfc: potential improvement of html display's css logic i propose to remove repetition of css styling in . every time that a cell in a jupyter notebook (or similar) uses the html display method, a new css block is added to the dom. this happens in ( but css is static and the styling rules don't change between estimators. that means that the in the css file ( is not needed i think. and we could cache the css file so it's loaded and added to the dom only once. this way we improve performance by avoiding: - dom bloat with many duplicate css rules. - browser memory waste. - less parsing overhead by the browser (browser processing several times identical styles). - repeated template substitution through string processing in . the same thing happens with the javascript script, it is added to the dom with each cell that uses a html display. but the script could be added just once at the bottom of the dom. note: the container id in is still needed as it is used by javascript - it's used to construct the fully qualified name of parameters. but i would add a new class name (to this file) because we still need a css selector in . i have done local tests that seem to work. if i am missing something about the purpose of multiple css blocks, i would love to know. wdyt? -xiao (or anybody) edit: crossing out points above, but keeping the last one. screenshot showing issue one can see that for each estimator, there is styling and a script.", 'attribute docstring does not show properly when there is a property with the same name describe the issue linked to the documentation when a is documented by a docstring and when the corresponding fitted attribute with the same name is also documented in the docstring of the class, the documentation only displays the first line of the docstring of the . the name of the property is also not properly rendered. for example of is displayed like this in the documentation: ! image ( although its docstring is: ! image ( and the docstring of the is: ! image ( this was probably introduced here : 30989 suggest a potential alternative fix one solution is to remove the docstring of the property, in which case the docstring of the attribute will be rendered properly. but it would have to be done in all such cases. i discovered it while working on randomforestclassifier.featureimportances that suffers from the same issue. cc: what do you think would be the right way to document an attribute coming from a property?']

Name: 8_estimator_estimators_attributes_fitted
Representative_Docs: ["yes, for the metric it could reuse the effective one, but there's no similar logic for weights so far. and it's like that in many estimators. so what i'm saying is that i believe that the inteded behavior so far has been to be reliable between construction and fit . to come back to your original questions: is the object state after fitting meant to be alterable by .setparams()? it's on a case by case basis. there are cases where changing a parameter after fit: - would break predict (could be considered a bug and be fixed) - would have no effect - would have an effect on predict and work as expected. that can be seen as a feature. so i think that we don't want to prevent the user from doing it but it's a do it at your own risk situation. an undefined behavior in short. the setparams description in the glossary could be improved to reflect that. and if so, should that trigger re-calculations of fitted attributes for example? this one, definitely not. the fitted attributes are the ones obtained from fit with the initial constructor parameters. changing the parameters to obtain new fitted attributes requires fitting again.", "hi, i d like to work on this issue. i ve reviewed the current behavior of 'setparams', the implementation in 'baseestimator', the developer guide, and related issues prs. there doesn t appear to be any existing documentation that explains what should happen when 'setparams' is called after an estimator has already been fitted, nor any ongoing work addressing this. summary of findings: - 'setparams' updates only hyperparameters and does not modify fitted attributes (those with a trailing ''). - calling it after 'fit' can leave the estimator in an inconsistent state, since learned attributes are not recomputed. - the developer guide and api docs do not currently describe this post fit behavior or provide guidance for estimator authors. - no related prs or issues seem to cover this gap. proposed approach: - add a clarification to the 'baseestimator.setparams' docstring explaining that: - it does not invalidate or recompute fitted attributes, - changing parameters after fitting may lead to inconsistencies, - users should call again if they want the estimator to reflect the updated parameters. - add a short subsection to the 'developing estimators' guide describing the expected behavior when parameters are changed after fitting, and advising estimator authors not to rely on 'setparams' to trigger recomputation. if this direction sounds good, i ll proceed with a pr.", "hi, i d like to work on this issue. i ve reviewed the current behavior of 'setparams', the implementation in 'baseestimator', the developer guide, and related issues prs. there doesn t appear to be any existing documentation that explains what should happen when 'setparams' is called after an estimator has already been fitted, nor any ongoing work addressing this. summary of findings: 'setparams' updates only hyperparameters and does not modify fitted attributes (those with a trailing ''). calling it after 'fit' can leave the estimator in an inconsistent state, since learned attributes are not recomputed. the developer guide and api docs do not currently describe this post fit behavior or provide guidance for estimator authors. no related prs or issues seem to cover this gap. proposed approach: add a clarification to the 'baseestimator.setparams' docstring explaining that: it does not invalidate or recompute fitted attributes, changing parameters after fitting may lead to inconsistencies, users should call again if they want the estimator to reflect the updated parameters. add a short subsection to the 'developing estimators' guide describing the expected behavior when parameters are changed after fitting, and advising estimator authors not to rely on 'set_params' to trigger recomputation. if this direction sounds good, i ll proceed with a pr. i understood from the earlier message that the second point in your list is not intended behavior - i.e. estimators should make sure that the call to sets all the necessary state in internal or protected attributes, so that calling wouldn't leave it in an inconsistent state."]

locally with all random seeds ( one would do:', 'looks like a test too sensitive to the random seed. i confirmed it locally and found errors for a few other random seeds.', "rfc question global random seed testing as a developer, i more and more doubt the added value of our global random seed test setup. ideally, i do not want to bear the mental load of: - running tests locally with all random seeds ( ) - add a commit with message just to trigger ci cd to run with all seeds. recently, ci cd often fails because of runs with all random seeds. this not only annoys but actually slows down development. it seems to be mainly how takes care of those. what is the actual benefit of it? which bugs have we found this way? on top, we have the open issue 22827, which seems like a never ending story. i mostly don't see the added value of the changed tests. see discussion in 33021: by general comment: as a developer, i find our test setup too complicated. even when running tests with all rng seeds locally, one still needs a commit to trigger the cd pipeline with all seeds. i would prefer to not need to think about test random seeds at all. by personally i am less and less convinced that our globalrandomseed setup is worth its maintenance cost. i do understand why it exists, i.e. catch numerical instability or avoid that a test pass only because we chose a lucky random seed. at the same time this setup has a maintenance cost and i am not sure we have actually seen any of the benefit. maybe i don't remember the few cases where it allowed us to notice a numerical instability or maybe it's the kind of silent usefulness that makes you write a more robust test in the first place and or catch numerical instability bugs when you write the test. to be explicit about the maintenance costs: intermittent ci error tracking. i am probably the main person that seems to care about and adds some noise in the ci. i would say globalrandomseed are the main source of intermittent errors even if there are others, like conda-forge download error, or intermittent segmentation fault on free-threaded. ad-hoc pr raising atol and or rtol where nobody really has a super clear idea whether this is ok or not to raise it or it would need a deeper look and generally we merge anyway. new globalrandomseed issues keep appearing, the pr ci only checks a single random seed, and it's only afterwards that you notice issues in automatically opened issues where the global random seed is random on each daily schedule run. to answer your point i don't know how to make our test setup simpler with our current set of constraints so i am proposing to remove one constraint (without too much hope to be honest). in an ideal infinite-resources world (which we don't live in, remember ), i think we should have some kind of fuzzing-like tests that lives outside the main ci workflow, tweaks the random seeds, and reports the most problematic tests (maybe they fail for a high number of seeds, maybe the difference is big for values not near 0, or some better criterion). just to be explicit, this ideal scenario is super unlikely to happen."]

Representative_Docs: ["allows finer costs to be taken into account in learning describe the workflow you want to enable i am generally trying to take into account costs in learning. the set-up is as follows: a statistical learning problem with usuall x and y, where y is imbalanced (roughly 1 of ones). i also have costs matrices c (see below). scikit learn usually offers wights parameters where you can set up weights matching imbalance. so the weights are depending on the target. assigning weights will transform the log loss into weighted log loss as seen below. text log loss - frac 1 n sum i 1 n left yi log( hat y i) (1 - yi) log(1 - hat y i) right text weighted log loss - frac 1 n sum i 1 n w yi left yi log( hat y i) (1 - yi) log(1 - hat y i) right as you see the weights w is constant on each classes and only depends on yi . i am generally looking for specifying the weights in terms of errors costs. more specifically, i have costs matrixes c associated with: - c 1,1 , cost associated with true positives (correctly identified positives) - c 0,1 , cost associated with false positives (type 1 error) - c 1,0 , cost associated with false negatives (type 2 error) - c 0,0 , cost associated with true negatives (correctly identified negatives) with three sub-cases: 1) c yi,1,1 , c yi,0,1 , c yi,1,0 , c yi,0,0 depends only on classes, typically i have classifications costs for each classes (8 parameters in total) 2) c i,1,1 , c i,0,1 , c i,1,0 , c i,0,0 depends on instances, so i have four values for each instances. 3) c i,1,1 , c i,0,1 , c i,1,0 , c i,0,0 depends both on instances and models outputs hat y i . i think the most generic approach would be to take: c 0,1 c 1,1 0 as we predict the rare event and refuse the relation c 0,0 hat y iwi as we accept the relation and charge proportionally to the estimated risk times some instance notional. c 1,0 hat y iwi-wi as we charge proportionally to risks but loose the notional. it appears the cost function can be rewritten as: however, it doesn't really seems standard to passes those costs matrices to scikit-learn. describe your proposed solution for the different subcases: 1) ideally, the weights in estimators (class weights) should be able to take into account cost matrices. 2) ideally, the weights in estimators (sample weights) should be able to take into account cost matrices. 3) seems trickier as it would requires to accept customs losses with the costs implemented as a function of hat y . maybe this is not possible to make a generic implementation. but i think the case mentioned it would works, notably accessing classes instances weights. additional context no response_", 'minibatchkmeans not handling sample weights as expected describe the bug following up from pr 29907, we minibatchindices are not resampled with weights but instead weights are passed on to the subsequent minibatchstep which returns resulting in sample weight equivalence not being respected (i.e., Name: 14_distance_clustering_algorithm_algorithms
Representative_Docs: ["thanks for opening this issue. from a quick look at the paper, it seems that this clustering algorithm is primarily meant for graphs where each node is connected to a few other nodes. in the context of scikit-learn clustering, the graph structure is not already known a priori: nodes are typically described by feature vectors (rows in ) on which it is possible to define distances and then clustering algorithm typically perform distance-based decisions to tell if two records nodes below to the same cluster or not. some clustering algorithms in scikit-learn (e.g. ) do take an optional graph connectivity structure as an extra constraint, but it's rather an exception than a rule. other algorithms accept a (potentially truncated) precomputed affinity matrix, but they also accept working on raw feature vectors. my questions are: - how would you fit the louvain clustering algorithm to the scikit-learn api? - do you consider that each row in is a node connected to all other nodes with a strength that relates to the inverse of the distances? - does the leiden clustering paper propose a standard way to do that? (i ctrl-f'ed the world distance and could not find any occurence). - wouldn't a graph processing library such as networkx better suited for this algorithm it is vastly superior to most clustering algorithms currently included in scikit learn (on non-trivial datasets) how do you compare clustering results for the applications you have in mind? in particular, do you have references that compare the results of clustering algorithm implemented in scikit-learn to leiden clustering?", "enh adding kmodes and kprototypes clustering algorithms describe the workflow you want to enable currently, scikit-learn users working with datasets that contain categorical features (e.g., , , ) face a significant hurdle. the standard practice is to use one-hot encoding before applying algorithms like k-means. this workflow is problematic because: 1. high-dimensionality: it drastically increases the dimensionality of the dataset (the curse of dimensionality ). 2. sparsity: it creates a sparse matrix that is computationally inefficient and poorly suited for distance-based algorithms like k-means, which are designed for dense, numerical data. 3. interpretability: the resulting clusters are based on a transformed version of the data, making the centroids and the cluster logic difficult to interpret in terms of the original categorical features. the workflow i want to enable is a seamless and native experience for clustering categorical and mixed data: for fully categorical data: a user should be able to call directly, without any pre-processing. for mixed data types: a user should be able to call , where they simply specify which columns are categorical. the algorithm would then automatically use an appropriate dissimilarity measure for each data type. this integrates categorical clustering directly into the robust and familiar scikit-learn api, eliminating the need for external dependencies and inefficient pre-processing. describe your proposed solution i propose implementing the well-established k-modes and k-prototypes algorithms as new classes within the module. these algorithms are the canonical equivalents of k-means for categorical and mixed data, respectively. proposed api design (following scikit-learn conventions): implementation plan: 1. api consistency: the classes will inherit from and , ensuring full compatibility with the scikit-learn ecosystem (e.g., , , , , ). 2. performance: the initial implementation will be in python with numpy, with a clear path for future optimization using cython if needed, following the same pattern as the codebase. 3. validation: input validation will use scikit-learn's existing utilities ( , ). the parameter will be rigorously validated against the input data's shape. 4. comprehensive testing: i will write a full suite of tests in and , covering functionality, convergence, input validation, and consistency against known results. 5. documentation: i will provide detailed docstrings with mathematical formulations (modes, cost function) and create examples demonstrating the use cases in the gallery. describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant the main alternative for users today is to use the third-party package. while it is a good implementation, relying on it has several drawbacks: 1. ecosystem fragmentation: it forces users to leave the integrated scikit-learn environment for a specific task. this breaks workflow consistency and requires managing an external dependency. 2. inconsistency: the api, while similar, is not identical to scikit-learn's. this creates a learning curve and potential for errors. 3. limited compatibility: it does not integrate as smoothly with scikit-learn's powerful ecosystem tools like , , or in libraries like spark mllib. users cannot build a simple pipeline like . 4. maintenance and visibility: as an external library, it has less visibility and a smaller pool of potential contributors compared to a feature within scikit-learn itself. users may not even be aware it exists. another alternative, one-hot encoding k-means, is fundamentally flawed for the reasons described in the first section (high dimensionality, sparsity, poor interpretability). therefore, the best alternative is a native implementation that provides a unified, consistent, and efficient experience for the scikit-learn community. additional context mathematical foundation: the algorithms are well-established with strong academic foundations. the key references are: k-modes: huang, z. (1998). extensions to the k-means algorithm for clustering large data sets with categorical values. data mining and knowledge discovery. k-prototypes: huang, z. (1997). clustering large data sets with mixed numeric and categorical values. proceedings of the first pacific-asia conference on knowledge discovery and data mining. the cost function for k-modes minimizes the sum of dissimilarities using a simple matching function (0 for same category, 1 for different). k-prototypes combines this with the euclidean distance for numerical features. personal commitment: i am a mathematician from spbu and ml engineer at sber with a deep understanding of these algorithms and the scikit-learn codebase. i am fully committed to implementing this feature to the project's highest standards, including comprehensive tests, documentation, and examples. i am prepared to iterate on the code based on feedback from the maintainers. scope: this enhancement fits perfectly within the scope of scikit-learn's module, filling a clear and important gap for a common data type (categorical data) that is currently underserved.", "add weighted euclidean distance metric describe the workflow you want to enable the workflow i want to enable is the ability for users to easily incorporate feature importance into distance-based algorithms like clustering (e.g., kmeans) and nearest neighbors (e.g., kneighborsclassifier). currently, scikit-learn allows users to define custom distance metrics, but there is no built-in support for weighted distance metrics, which are essential when certain features are more important than others. example workflow: a user has a dataset where some features are more relevant than others (e.g., in customer segmentation, age and income might be more important than the number of children). the user wants to use a clustering algorithm like kmeans or a nearest neighbors algorithm like kneighborsclassifier but needs to account for the varying importance of features. the user specifies a vector of weights corresponding to the importance of each feature. the algorithm uses the weighted euclidean distance metric to compute distances, ensuring that more important features have a greater influence on the results. describe your proposed solution i propose adding a weighted euclidean distance metric to scikit-learn as a built-in distance metric. this will allow users to specify feature weights directly, making it easier to incorporate feature importance into distance-based algorithms. key components of the solution: 1. new class: - add a weightedeuclideandistance class to the sklearn.metrics.pairwise module. this class will accept a vector of weights during initialization. - it will compute the weighted euclidean distance between two points using the formula: d(x, y) sqrt( summation from i 1 to n of wi (xi - yi) squared ) where wi are the user-defined weights. describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant no response additional context why this feature is needed: - feature importance: in many real-world datasets, not all features are equally important. for example, in medical diagnosis, certain biomarkers might be more relevant than others. a weighted distance metric allows users to account for this. - - ease of use: while scikit-learn allows users to define custom distance metrics, this requires writing additional code and can be error-prone. a built-in weighted distance metric would simplify the process and make it more accessible to users. - - alignment with scikit-learn s design: scikit-learn emphasizes ease of use and consistency. adding a weighted distance metric aligns with this philosophy by providing a simple, consistent way to incorporate feature importance into distance-based algorithms. existing alternatives: - custom distance functions: users can define custom distance functions using the metric parameter. however, this requires additional coding and lacks the convenience of a built-in solution. - - feature weighting via preprocessing: users can scale features by their importance before applying distance-based algorithms. however, this approach is less intuitive and can be cumbersome. potential impact: - this feature would benefit users in domains like bioinformatics, finance, and customer analytics, where feature importance is critical. - - it would also make scikit-learn more competitive with other machine learning libraries that offer weighted distance metrics."]

"histgradientboostingclassifier regressor 15x slowdown on small data problems compared to disabled openmp threading this problem was first described as part of 14306, but i think it might make sense to open a dedicated issue for the particular problem of small data shapes. the fundamental problem seems to be that the openmp threadpool overhead is frequently detrimental to performance on. note that openmp threading is enabled by default in general in scikit-learn. here are the relative durations of running cross-validation on this model on data with various size with and without enabling threads: ! image ( ! image ( ! image ( speed-up are measured in relative improvement in fit speed compared to a sequential fit (multi-threading disabled). this was collected on an apple m1 cpu with 4 performance cores and 4 efficiency cores with llvm-opemp installed from conda-forge. here are my conclusions: - using threads can cause a huge slowdown on the smallest problems (15x slower than when running with the threads disabled); - openmp threading becomes benefitial only with large datasets (more than 100k data points with hundreds of dimensions); - using the 4 efficiency cores is almost always detrimental. for intel amd x86 cpus we already disable smt hyperthreading for a similar reason (see 26082). but i don't know how to do something similar for efficiency cores in platform-agnostic way; - we should probably disable multi-threading, or at least reduce the number of threads with a heuristic that depends on the data shape such as: - implementing sample-wise parallelism ( could probably help a bit on some case ( , but i still think openmp threading should be disabled on the smallest problems. - results will probably also be impacted if we merge 27168. Name: 18_loss_absolute_absolute error_median
Representative_Docs: ["doc: clarify the documentation for the loss functions used in gbrt, and absolute error in particular. describe the bug from my understanding, currently there is no way to minimize the mae (mean absolute error). quantile regression with quantile 0.5 will optimize for the median absolute error. this would be different from optimizing the mae when the conditional distribution of the response variable is not symmetrically-distributed. what i expect - using should optimize for the mean of absolute errors. - using should optimize for the median of absolute errors. what happens both give the same results - using optimizes for the median of absolute errors - using optimizes for the median of absolute errors suggested actions if this is intended behavior: - feel free to close this issue marked as resolved. - kindly add a note in the documentation that absolute error optimizes for median absolute error, not mean absolute error as absoluteerror is not very clear. - i would appreciate if there was more explanation regarding on using custom loss functions 21614. this way, we could optimize for mean absolute error, median absolute error, log cosh, etc. as per the requirement. note i have tried my best to go through the documentation prior to creating this issue. i am a fresh graduate in computer science, and if you believe this issue is not well-framed due to a misunderstanding of my concepts, kindly advise me and i'll work on it. steps code to reproduce expected results median and mean of absolute errors should give different results for a log-normally distributed response. hence, the predictions should be different from each other, and the difference of their predictions, should total as a non-zero value. actual results predictions by both models are the same, which can be seen in the difference of their predictions, totaling as 0. versions", 'please point us to the specific part of the documentation that was not clear and suggest a way to improve it. otherwise i think we can close. problem the term squarederror and absoluteerror is not very clear: ie, is it mean squared error or median squared error; mean absolute error or median absolute error. the documentation should make it clear that it is mean squared error and mean absolute error. specific part of the documentation loss squarederror , absoluteerror , gamma , poisson , quantile , default squarederror the loss function to use in the boosting process. note that the squared error , gamma and poisson losses actually implement half least squares loss , half gamma deviance and half poisson deviance to simplify the computation of the gradient. furthermore, gamma and poisson losses internally use a log-link, gamma requires y 0 and poisson requires y 0. quantile uses the pinball loss. suggestion the loss function to use in the boosting process. note that the squared error , gamma and poisson losses actually implement half least squares loss , half gamma deviance and half poisson deviance to simplify the computation of the gradient. furthermore, gamma and poisson losses internally use a log-link, gamma requires y 0 and poisson requires y 0. quantile uses the pinball loss. absoluteerror refers to mean absolute error; for other variants of absolute error, you need to write your own loss function, squarederror refers to mean squared error; for other variants of squared error, you need to write your own loss function', "absoluteerror refers to mean absolute error; for other variants of absolute error, you need to write your own loss function, squarederror refers to mean squared error; for other variants of squared error, you need to write your own loss function i would not write that: for all loss functions, it's always computed as the sum (or equivalently the mean) of the individual loss function computed on each data point in the training set: this is the case for absolute and squared error but also for quantile, poisson, gamma... furthermore, we should not advertise for custom loss functions in the doc as there is no official public api to do so in scikit-learn."]

Name: 19_bins_calibration_bin_curve
Representative_Docs: ["add 'quantile' strategy to describe the workflow you want to enable choose between different binning strategies ( uniform fixed width bins, quantile equal mass bins) to estimate the calibration curve. describe your proposed solution adding in a new or option to switch between the two binning strategies. we can for now just add the option and change the default in a follow up pr. we can also add (in the same or follow up pr) the option to implement the corp approach as advocated in 23132. describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant no response additional context there are multiple issues prs on implementing calibration metrics (for example the expected calibration error, the brier score decomposition) and improving reliability diagrams: 18268, 11096, 22233, 21774, 23767, 23132 there are two main ways to estimate the calibration curve (and metrics): 1. using binning, either uniform (fixed-width bins) or quantile (equal mass bins) 2. using isotonic regression, this is the corp ( approach, see 23132 for the binning case, the quality of the estimate in the binning depends crucially on 1a. the number of bins, it seems that is best, this is tackled by 33856 1b. the binning strategy, it seems that quantile is better, see roelofs et al ( or nixon et al ( reading through the literature, i feel that the best estimator for the calibration curve metrics is not yet settled. based on the experiments reported in the corp ( paper, the isotonic regression seems the most accurate. however there are ways to debias the binned estimates for the brier score decomposition that we could investigate, see ferro and fricker, 2012 ( also, we might want the different options with each its own benefits: fixed-width bins to visually compare different curves or put them in a dataframe, equal mass bins to get a more reliable curve with a manageable length ( ), isotonic regression for its accuracy but at the expense of a crowded curve (length ). overall, having the different options seems to me the best way for further implementations of the calibration metrics or curve, without having to commit to one option over another. cc", "i appreciate the discussion around the diagnostic usage of this tool, but i'd like to offer my perspective on why a statistically driven cubic root rule is preferrable to a fixed value like 5 or 10. 1. is an estimator, not just a plot it is not uncommon to use calibrationcurve beyond diagnostics tools. the e y z z . beyond visualization, it is a foundational step for - recalibration: mapping predicted probabilities to actual probabilities (e.g., zadrozny and elkan 2001 ( - ece estimation: serving as a plug-in estimate for calibration error (e.g. - hypothesis testing: determine if a model is calibrated (e.g., lee et al., 2023 ( kipnis 2026 ( the quality of the function estimation of h carries over to these applications. 2. usefulness of diagnostic tools depends on its accuracy even as a diagnostic tool, a visualization is only as useful as it accuracy. an overly biased estimator due to fixed, low bin counts can create false diagnostic by blurring out the refined structure of the model's calibration (e.g. figure 3(a) of lee et al., 2023 ( if i understand correctly, 's concern is that cubic-root rule might produce thousands of bins and lead to a noisy visualization that's hard to interpret. the noisy (wiggly calibration curve) is a result of using too few samples per bin. this is exactly the other extreme the cubic rule is designed to prevent. with increasing number of samples, the cubic rule optimally balance the increased statistical power to the sample size per bin and the number of bins. for example, to reach 1,000 bins, a user would need 1,000,000,000 samples, but each bin still contains 1 million samples, resulting in a highly stable, high-resolution curve that reveals refined structures, not a noisy plot. if a user collected a billion data points, they expect to see the refined structure of their model. in my work on large-scale recommendation platforms, the calibration of click-through-rate is checked on millions of data points and hundreds of bins for better resolution. a 10-bin plot for a billion sample would be a massive waste of information, potentially hiding local miscalibration that are statistically significant reveals at finer resolutions. 3. mathematical grounding we now do have a good understanding of what hyperparameter minimizes the binning method's risk. the following as a function of sample size n and bins b . i agree with that switching the default to 10 doesn't meet the goal. bin number 5 and 10 corresponds to the first two columns, which are clearly suboptimal than the path going down the valley. and what's beautiful about this theory is it's distribution-free under weak nonparametric assumptions - meaning most of real-world distribution have this cubic-root type of surface. my suggestions i am with that there should be a good documentation how works. maybe we should implement options first with more descriptive names before defining a strategy to select among them. following 's suggestions, i'd suggest at least adding option implementing the simple cubic root option. regarding 's comment, i think either or would work as they are statistically equivalent and robust in programming (works for all positive integer n ). but i don't recommend implementing for the option which could be considered for as suggested. regarding the default, i still think is a good option - it is simple, intrinsically robust (works for all positive integer n ), provably optimal, ideal for visualization, and beneficial to other potential downstream applications . i want to understand if this aligns with sklearn's design and development.", "ux 's naive use can lead to very confusing results the naive use of parameter silently leads to degenerate, noisy results when some bins have with a few data points. for instance, look at the variability obtained by displaying for calibration curve of a fitted model evaluated on various resampling with 50 data points in total using the uniform strategy when using and the default : ! image ( this problem can easily happen with the default if the test data is not large enough. i think this class should warn the user whenever it generates bins with lower than 10 data points per bin. a typical user will only get one of the curves above and not suspect that it's just noise without manually plotting the others by random resampling. note that i chose a minimal test set to make the problem catastrophic above, but it can happen with larger sample sizes, in particular with the uniform strategy, in particular on imbalanced datasets. updated recommendations edit: based on the discussion below, here are my recommendations to address this issue: - display a confidence interval by default to reflect our uncertainty on the calibration curve estimate (using the clopper-pearson analytical ci from scipy looks both reliable and cheap to compute) - we set by default but also introduce to automatically merge the smallest pair of neighboring bins until that constraint it met. - we would not warn, unless the resulting merging leads to less than 3 bins for instance, - we could keep if we like, but i wouldn't mind switching to either. original description and suggestion in such cases, this warning could recommend to: - use (which is a bit less likely to generate near empty, unstable bins); - decrease the number of bins; - increase the number of data points in the test set. beyond warnings, we could also offer the user extra-options to assess the stability of the calibration curve. there are two cases: - for a fixed trained estimator, we could estimate the uncertainty in the estimation of its calibration curve induced by the finite size of the evaluation set (irrespective of whether the training procedure is stable or not) by resampling the test set (with replacement): we could optionally offer an option to aggregate those curves by displaying a shaded region derived from the vertical quantiles (e.g. 0.05-0.95) of the interpolated y-curve values at a fine grid of x values. see also the related issue on sampling uncertainties in performance curves: 25856. - to assess the uncertainty of the calibration of a full fitting procedure (uncertainty induced by both the finite training set size, the training algorithm itself (impacted by the choice of hyperparams) and the finite evaluation set size), we could also offer to plot calibration curves from cv results (see 21211 to implement ). on the opposite, if all the bins have more than 1000 data points and , we could suggest the user to increase n_bins to 10 or more to get a finer estimation of the calibration curve. maybe this recommendation is better to be set in a docstring rather than a warning. i think we could start simple by opening a first pr to issue warnings for the nearly empty bin problem (with an option to silence them)."]
