Representative_Docs: ["i played around with this a bit more. here is a version with the class contour lines after the level fix (it simply plots the boundaries on top of the class ones (i left the in for color reference)) and have an parameter, which will smooth the lines a bit when set to , which i added as the default. regarding the colors not matching a single class with : we could easily plot them in a single color like this (i hard-coded it to with here, not sure if we should leave it colored for , though). continuing what also already mentioned above: we might need to separate the into separate (also with the option of . the only problem i don't know how to solve is the line width, which will increase for multiple overlapping contour lines. but since this mainly happens when is very high, i'm not sure how big a concern this should be. one other thing i noticed about the grid resolution is that we assume quadratic plots here (which is the case in the examples above, but might not necessarily be the case in other scenarios): an option could be to use something like this, which could require adapting the default value (though i'm not sure what the best option would be here and what to base this decision on): the plots above were created with this approach and . i will open a draft pr for the first version tomorrow, but i already wanted to get this out here in case you have a moment left to think about this, .", 'thank you for your input on this! to clarify, there are two points we are talking about here: 1. the default colormap (or colors) to use i think, from the discussion above, we agree that might not be ideal. i like the petroff colors and will look at ways to integrate them, once we agree on the second point below. 2. the sampling mechanism this is what you also observed above ( . if you pass a and with classes, matplotlib will sample evenly distributed colors from the colormap. this is where we handle things differently in , which will instead select the first colors when a qualitative colormap is passed to . but this difference is only for qualitative colormaps, for continuous colormaps, the user can simply pass viridis (or whichever other colormap) both to and to in any other mpl plotting function on top, and the colors will match. i think this might overcomplicate things, so i would vote for handling colormaps the same way in both cases (i.e., sampling evenly). having said that, we can still use the petroff colors as the default as you suggested , and the user can then also reuse them with so, if you agree, i would 1. implement the petroff colors as the default for as you suggested 2. change the sampling behaviour for all colormaps to sample evenly, regardless of qualitative or continuous.', "responding to some of the comments from 31693, as well here to have the discussion in a single place. with regard to the 2 issues below: there is a detailed handling of colors using the multiclasscolors parameter, but this is only taken into account when multiclass predictproba or decision_function are used. the distinction is made based on the output shape ( and predict will always have self.response.ndim 2, but can still be multiclass. (from what is the reason for handling colors by different parameters in the binary (currently including multiclass predict) and multiclass cases? (from above) i can't think of a reason why multiclass should be handled differently. i think it was an oversight as 29797 was originally about adding support for plotting of multiclass and ( was already supported), we when the parameter was added later in the pr, we just didn't think about . what is the reason for handling colors by different parameters in the binary (currently including multiclass predict) and multiclass cases? again, just because we were thinking about multiclass. i would be happy to deprecate for (or maybe ?). current input options: i think the default value currently ( tab10 ( would be reasonable for binary case, as the first 2 colors are blue and orange. i do think we should advise against use of a string mpl colormap for use in the binary case in the docs, but probably fine if allow it. --- i see several issues: 1. current issue: ignores 2. - mpl bug when number of classes 7, that is propagated 3. zooming in on an area of the plot means we calculate the responses only for a subset of the data points, which doesn't necessarily include all classes, and results in inconsistent colors: 4. - (or maybe ?) it seems that items 1,2,3 all would benefit from knowing the full number of classes, which we can obtain from the fitted (and would avoid expensive calls to , which is done in 32867). there are 2 ways we could do this, pass number of classes (i.e. , which we currently use in ) to (probably via class instantiation in ), or move the color and level logic (currently handled by ) to . i think the former (passing ) to plot would be preferable (it certainly would reduce the git diff) but you may have a better feel when you implement this. also i think issue 1 should be addressed first - it seems a more important bug to fix, and the restructure (i.e. passing of would be useful for subsequent issues)."]

Name: 21_solver_newton_warm_intercept
Representative_Docs: ["frisch-newton interior point solver for quantile regression describe the workflow you want to enable hi scikit-learn devs! over at pyfixest ( we have implemented a frisch-newton interior point solver to fit quantile regressions. the algorithm goes back to work from koenker. in practice, we have followed koenker and ng a frisch-newton algorithm for sparse quantile regression . ( the code is licensed under mit and available here ( we ( ) have collected some benchmarks here ( - the fn solver seems to outperform the scikit default solver by an order of a magnitude. would you be interested in a pr that adds the fn solver as a new estimation method to the quantile regression class? we've also implemented algorithms from chernozhukov et al ( can drastically speed up estimation of the entire quantile regression process. all the best, alex describe your proposed solution i open a pr and add a new solver fn to . describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant no response additional context related to", "yes, this step is done after the optimization procedure (using steps) to make all unpenalized parameters unique, i.e., sum to zero. as i explained, this destroys the equivalence of same number of steps with warm start vs all steps at once. it is even worse: converges, but calling 6 or more time does not converge. so what is more important: making repeated warm-start calls to equivalent to one with many steps. keeping current symmetrizing behavior. the newton-cholesky solver is not a sgd solver nor a minibatch solver and has no method. you should give it at least some number of iterations and if you prefer an even higher accuracy later, you can use a warm start fit to improve. for me, this behavior is fine because i don't have a use case to call repeated fit with small number of iterations. i don't follow. if it were the case that the only thing going on is the centering of intercepts, then the predicted probabilities would be the same regardless of whether the intercepts sum to zero or to some other number, so long as all of them are off by the same constant. for example: the only way in which an exact newton update would give the same result for coefficients given the same start, is if predictions from which gradients and hessians were the same, which is the case whenever the intercepts are equivalent up to an arbitrary constant shift - i.e.: hence, centering of the intercepts should not change the end result in terms of predicted probabilities. yet in this case, the coefficients are matching, which means that the intercepts are being used correctly prior to the newton update, but are then being incorrectly modified in a non-equivalent way afterwards. if they received the same update and get centered after the update, the result would still be equivalent between the warm and cold start, at the very least in terms of predicted probabilities.", "yes, this step is done after the optimization procedure (using steps) to make all unpenalized parameters unique, i.e., sum to zero. as i explained, this destroys the equivalence of same number of steps with warm start vs all steps at once. it is even worse: converges, but calling 6 or more time does not converge. so what is more important: - making repeated warm-start calls to equivalent to one with many steps. - keeping current symmetrizing behavior. the newton-cholesky solver is not a sgd solver nor a minibatch solver and has no method. you should give it at least some number of iterations and if you prefer an even higher accuracy later, you can use a warm start fit to improve. for me, this behavior is fine because i don't have a use case to call repeated fit with small number of iterations. side remark: one step of newton-cholesky and the finalize actually puts the parameters in such a bad region that it practically never recovers from it (if one keeps warm starting)."]

Name: 25_histogram_trees_binning_sort
Representative_Docs: ["i'm pretty sure histogram based split is not compatible with mae criterion i don t think this is an obstacle. the features x are binned, not the (unbinned) target y. the median is computed on y. also, it works for hgb and the line search step computes quantiles medians on y. sorry, my message was not super clear: it's totally possible to do binning mae criterion. but i maintain that it's impossible to adapt the histogram-based splitting algorithm to the mae criterion. what we mean by histogram-based splitting algorithm is the following algorithm: for most criteria, binning lets you find the best split in two efficient steps (for each feature): - compute some kind of aggregates per bin (sum of targets per bin, counts per bin, ...). this is what we call a histogram in the hgb code. this step is o(n) and is highly optimized in the hgb code. - use this histogram to find the best split in o(nbins). this works because, for instance, sumleft sum of binsum for bins on the left. you don't have such property for the median. this is efficient because it's o(n) overall. moreover, it has a fairly good memory access pattern locality and a small memory footprint, so it can be efficiently parallelized over features. (i'll let thumbs up if my explanation of histogram-based splits is correct.) now my claim: it's not possible to apply this algorithm for the mae criterion; moreover, the best complexity you can get for finding the best split with the mae criterion is o(n log n) - or o(n n_bins), which is good if you have only a few bins but not great for 256 bins.", "some thoughts: binning mae criterion: - i'm pretty sure histogram based split is not compatible with mae criterion (try to think about how computing the median in a histogram-way , pretty sure it's not feasible).suel - because of that, binning would not bring a significant speed up for mae (because mae splitting run time is dominated by anyway, so making the sort partitioning faster is kinda pointless). it would be ok not to support binning mae criterion, it wouldn't bring a lot of value. ---- binning deep trees i said that histogram based splitting is not adapted for deep trees: well maybe this can be mitigated. and because it's incredibly fast for the first floors of the tree, the overall speed might be great. but the current implementation will not be easy to adapt, especially if we want to keep the s. i'm experimenting with my idea of o(n) bin-based sorting, and i think it's promising, we can gain a 10x speed-up. i guess histogram based splitting could be even faster though. the implementation i would has a good memory locality. --- binning other criteria: i need to give it some serious thinking to be sure, but i think other criteria (gini, log-loss, mse, poisson) are compatible with histogram based splitting, including multi-class and multi-output cases i think. ... might be a mess to implement though --- i noticed almost nothing scales well with the number of threads on my laptop. so i can't really do benchmarks with multi-threading :", "i don't think histogram-based splitting is suited for random forests because it's not adapted for deep trees. here is a small benchmark comparing splitting speed for hgb and random forests by fitting them in equivalent conditions: same number of trees, same trees depth, same number of threads processes. (warning: takes 12 minutes to run) results: observations: - hbg is quite slow for deep trees, especially when parallelism is enabled (the threads start-up time probably kills perf in this case). - the distribution has a big impacts on t(rf): it's because of the sort used in decision trees (3-way partitioning quick-sort, see section below). for the distribution g(1 3) (geometric distribution with p 1 3), this sort is very fast. - parallelism is not linearly improving performances. i guess memory bandwidth limits gain you get from parallelism? as fitting trees requires many memory accesses but not that many computations in comparison (something like 3 flops for 1 memory access i would say). edit: my laptop has just overall poor multi-threading performances, results are hard to interpret. ----- but i deeply agree that binning would make random forests much faster. i just think that you don't need histogram-based splitting, you can keep the current logic of sort then left- right swipe and just use the fact you're working with binned values to vastly speed-up the sort (o(n log n) - o(n)). i experimented quite a lot with sorting feature values for trees, and here are my thoughts. you have three types of sort that are relevant for decision trees: - 3-way partitioning quick-sort: the currently implemented sort in decision trees. o(n log n) but very efficient on features where a few values concentrate most of the samples (e.g. 90 of the samples have value 1, 2 or 3). - 2-way partitioning quick-sort: o(n log n), much faster for features with no duplicates (e.g. ), like 3-10x faster iirc. - count-based sorting: only works to sort integers in 0, ..., m-1 where m is not too big. complexity: o(n m). ultra fast compared to quick-sort when m n. this is what binning unlocks. you could also pre-process features to map values to their ranks to be able to use it. this would be very efficient for features with a small number of unique values. note that this sort isn't interesting for small nodes (which you'll get for deep trees), unless m is really small. i think the most promising opportunities for speeding up bagging-based tree ensembles lie in using a sorting strategy adapted to each feature. i believe we can achieve a 2 10 speed-up depending on the case if we implement these ideas: using ranks for low-cardinality features, binning for high-cardinality features, or switching to a two-way partitioning quicksort for the latter when appropriate."]

Name: 27_inheritance_constructor_inherit_code
Representative_Docs: ["i think removing the argument from and makes sense. changing the value of doesn't make sense for this class. however, right now inherits from and passes to the constructor. if we remove this constructor argument from we lose the ability to have this convenient way of making an . in addition i think it is fine (if a bit strange) for a user to . why i think it's confusing: because for instance a is absolutely not an extremely randomized tree regressor as the doc describes this model. i think there is a typo here, did you mean ?", "so this issue really happen for third-party developer: there, i could witness that is solving the issue. is reintroducing because we will still break code of people. so there is definitely a complexity because you need to convert that do inheritance into a that just add tag for the class itself. but i think that i got it right with the different test. it really provide a fully backward compatible solution. and i'm not sure that option would be enough. translating to a means that you will create some default values. since use the inheritance, having the default tags will wrongly override the class of a child class i think. edit: actually , i was thinking the walk in the mro reversed :).", "i'd rather make inherit from instead (it's not a mixin per say then anymore though). we certainly shouldn't fix the issue as proposed in 30480 since it breaks code if inheritance order is wrong. the code written here in the op is simply not supported , however, it's the kinda thing that was supported before, albeit dysfunctional and wrong. i'd suggest two things: - improve the inheritance order check test, to make sure if there's any of the mixins present, is also present, and call the test before trying to get tags - the issue here is coming from called in , which was a fix in this release too. we can make catch that particular exception, raise a deprecation warning, and return default tags, and the warning says that the code runs , but it's probably wrong."]

Name: 28_install_development_page_source

(i have not figure out the right way to do this )', "doc clearer linear get your development environment setup documentation !note this issue was opened with in mind. if you are looking for an issue to work on, please have a look at our contributing doc ( and in particular the section issues for new contributors ( related to the discussion in i think one useful thing that is easier than restructuring the doc would be to have a linear get your development environment set-up similar to that you can easily follow step by step. that would replace the building from source section ( right now you need to go back and forth between different places of the doc which is less than ideal. you would be able to chose your os and conda vs pip and you would get something that you can follow step by step. see rst code for the installing doc: from a cursory look, it looks like this is using tabs and some include in a few places to share some content. cc in case you find this interesting to work on and with whom i had a quick chat about this topic. don't hesitate if you need clarifications!", "as a general guideline, i would try to have something simple to follow for new contributors. i would reduce the number of possibilities so only editable install. people who for some reason (i can't only think of any) want non editable installs will figure it out by themselves. i would work first on getting a separate page that looks good, which in itself is already a bit of work, with the os and conda pip thing. about how it fits in the existing doc, tricky topic: for now my current thinking that would probably need to be refined: - the contributing page only has a short text with a link to the get your environment set-up page (or build from source which is more generic, honestly i don't mind too much). - it seems like the building from source in the install page could point to this link? - i would move the install the nightly wheels from scientific-python-nightly-wheels somewhere else (maybe the install page? maybe somewhere else to be defined) ... this is mostly useful for: - users who want to test whether a bug is in the development version without building from source - other package maintainers who want to test against our development version (in general inside their ci)"]

well-documented or obvious. ease of use for complex scorers: while is convenient for simple functions, creating more complex, stateful scorers that properly handle metadata routing by inheriting from an internal class is not a viable public pattern. proposed solutions (informed by source code): given that already has the foundational link to , the path forward might be less about building from scratch and more about exposing and refining existing mechanisms: 1. short-term - documentation clarification (leveraging existing functionality): action: immediately improve the documentation to review and refine its method and the way it handles the and method for easier subclassing. the goal is to allow users to primarily override an internal scoring method (like ) and use the inherited for for scorers part of the issue title and provides a solid, object-oriented way for users to build complex, metadata-aware scorers. it also naturally explains how works in this context. regarding expose : 's functionality is best exposed through classes that inherit it (like and this proposed public ), rather than exposing it directly for all users, as it's more of a mixin building block. path Name: 30_routing_metadata_metadata routing_object
Representative_Docs: ["add metadata routing params support in the predict method of describe the workflow you want to enable hello! i'm trying to use metadata routing with and however it is implemented for the method, not the one. i am wondering if there is a particular reason for not doing it on the predict function or if this is a feature that could be added. this would enable situations like the following, which currently gives an error: describe your proposed solution similar to the fit method, something like: however, i don't have enough understanding of the metadata routing implementation to know exactly what should be done. describe alternatives you've considered, if relevant no response additional context i tried to have a look at the history of prs issues to find a discussion around this point, but could not find it in the pr introducing the metadata routing to these estimators ( 28432).", "hi -stevens-haas, thanks for your thoughtful follow-up and for pinpointing these areas of confusion in the documentation! these are exactly the kinds of questions that help improve understanding for everyone, especially with newer concepts like metadata routing. let me try to clarify those two points based on my understanding: 1. on metadatarouter and router object : you're right to question the terminology here, as it can be a bit circular at first glance. - a router object is generally the component that does the routing. in scikit-learn, this would typically be an estimator (like randomforestclassifier) or a scorer object. these objects have methods (e.g., fit(), score(), transform()) for which metadata (like sampleweight) can be routed. - the metadatarouter object (which is what getmetadatarouting() returns, and is an instance of sklearn.utils.metadatarouting.routingmethodcontainer) is not the router object itself. instead, it's a data structure that describes the current routing configuration of a specific router object. - so, when the documentation says a metadatarouter instance stores and handles metadata routing for a router object, it means it holds the settings, rules, or configuration map for how that particular estimator or scorer is set up to handle metadata for its various methods. for example, myestimator.getmetadatarouting() would return a metadatarouter object that tells you which metadata myestimator is configured to request for its fit method, its predict method, etc. - think of the estimator as the router device and the metadatarouter object as its configuration sheet or routing table. 2. on the serialized version from getmetadatarouting(): this is another good catch regarding potentially ambiguous phrasing in the documentation. - when the docs mention that getmetadatarouting() returns a serialized version of this class for consumer-only classes, it's generally referring to the metadatarouter object (i.e., routingmethodcontainer instance) itself. - it's serialized in the sense that it's a structured python object that represents the complete routing information for the consumer (e.g., an estimator). it's not typically serialized in the sense of a pickle.dumps() string or a raw json string, nor is it usually a simple flat named tuple of attributes. - instead, the routingmethodcontainer is an object that internally holds more detailed structures, often dictionaries. these map method names (like fit , score ) to methodrouting objects, which in turn specify which metadata keys are requested (e.g., sampleweight true, mycustommeta false) for each parameter of that method. - so, the getmetadata_routing() method provides a programmatic way to inspect this structured object and understand the detailed metadata requirements and routing configuration of an estimator or scorer. essentially, it gives you an inspectable data structure representing the routing rules, rather than a binary blob. hope this helps clarify those points! let me know your thoughts.", "hi , looks like you've solved your own problem and came up with a working example for routing metadata to a custom transformer within a pipeline. (to also circumvent the futurewarning your transformer needs to expose some trained attribute such as . concerning your above questions: 1. without enabling metadata routing, lamentably you cannot pass your custom param to the dummytransformer using the syntax. this is why you see the error with the link to the metadata routing docs only there. i agree this is unfortunate, and this is one of the reasons why maintainers introduced the metadata routing functionality. 2. the typeerror: pipeline.fit_transform got unexpected argument(s) ... is due to not having set the and methods on your dummytransformer. 3. agreed this user guide on metadata routing is not super clear. metadata routing is still experimental. if you want to contribute further by making an issue that points out the most confusing parts of the documentation on metadata routing, you are more than welcome."]

another fix would be to manually mark all the tests that have openmp parallelism with , since it probably doesn't make sense to simultaneously have two levels of parallelism like that.", "i am getting some weird 'interactions' with and pytest-xdist when trying to run test in parallel ok this was due to how i ran my test. leaving this here in case someone else runs into this problem. initializing the estimator with some object that can be different across pytest-xdist workers leads to xdist errors. best to pass an estimator that will always be the same across workers", "using with multiple pytest workers leads to race describe the bug when using with several workers to run a test suite that uses as a fixture ( ) the dataset shape is sometimes wrong. for example i just had a run where - it should be something like 11000 samples for the train subset. some test functions will see the shorter dataset and some the full dataset. i think the problem is caused by using where each worker will download the dataset itself. the download itself will work in parallel (though wasteful) but then when the file gets d to the final location things get broken? or one of the workers sees a partial file somehow. this is the relevant code i'm wondering if the fix is to not use to create the filename to download to, but instead use a name like and check if that file exists before starting a download. that way only one process would start downloading the file. the problem is that we would need a function that will perform the check and creation of a path in an atomic operation. not sure that exists :- steps code to reproduce if you put this snippet into a file and run it with it reproduces a different version (i think) of the problem. make sure to delete the 20newsgroups file(s) from before every run (i use ) i originally found this by running a subset of the cuml testsuite with . you will sometimes observe failures caused by the fact that there are less classes in the dataset (changes from run to run) than priors (which are hardcoded to twenty) passed to the estimator. the explanation is that the random subset used by the test does not contain all the classes. i think because samples are stored in order in the file. so a test that uses a partially downloaded file only sees a subset. if you look at the size of it can be less than 13mb (which seems to be the correct size). this came up in the context of expected results the dataset always has the same size actual results shape of the dataset changes between test functions in one run. versions"]

Name: 34_narwhals_polars_dataframe_pandas
Representative_Docs: ['meta: use narwhals in codebase 31127 was merged and so we can now rely on narwhals. todo: - add entry for dataframe in glossary - extend user guide on data interoperability ( - remove use of - extend many pandas-only checks to dataframe maybes: - use narwhals dataframe operations instead of array operations - narhals-in, narwhals-out (for )', "what does, for polars support, narwhals gain us? we already have a decent support of polars. the main benefit i see is to have one code path that supports both pandas and polars. and open the door to toggle on other dataframe libraries. --- it would be great to use their dataframe-first expression api, but that brings us too far from our current way of doing things. status quo is: 1. ndarray - compute on ndarray 2. array-api array - compute on array-api array 3. dataframe - convert to ndarray - compute on ndarray if we add dataframe-first expression api for compute, it'll add a third code path, which i do not really like: 4. dataframe - compute using dataframe expression api (although 4. means we can have lazy dataframe support) --- that being said, i think there is a net win for vendoring narwhals to more easily support polars pandas at the same time. what is your plan for the future? would the switch to narwhals be done in a single pr? is it going to be a lengthy per-function estimator process like array api support (hopefully not )? for me, i would: 1. vendor narwhals and use 2. i suspect most of the changes are in and 3. there are a few places where we use the dataframe interchange, which most likely need to update to narwhals. from a user point of view, narhwals code change would be invisible to them and they get the same feature set.", "ok nobody was bold enough to raise concerns yet, so let me the first one to do it . being not very familiar with the space, i would need more information to first form an opinion and eventually try to reach a decision on this. i have to admit my default reaction is conservatism. adding a new dependency in scikit-learn has always been done with careful consideration about the trade-offs involved. one of my worry: i have to admit that from afar this ecosystem (to simplify let's say narwhals polars) moves very fast (which you know can also be a good thing ) and there seems to be an impedance mismatch with how fast the core scientific python ecosystem is moving. what does it bring us? this has probably been discussed in other issues but it would be nice to have it summarized in this issue where we are trying to make a decision. - what does depending on narwhals bring us? is it mostly to do pandas polars conveniently? are there any issues with our pandas polars implementation that depending on narwhals would fix? - are there many users who care about support for an other dataframe library than pandas or polars? - what kind of workflows would depending on narwhals unlock for you? what kind of work-around do you currently use? how will our code change? aka what does it cost us? - how much of our code will need to change? is it mostly a few (5-10) key functions? - what is your plan for the future? would the switch to narwhals be done in a single pr? is it going to be a lengthy per-function estimator process like array api support (hopefully not )? - not sure how much work this is, but it would help a lot if your wip pr was changing our code in a way that enables you to do something that wasn't possible before. this would help having some feeling how much change is needed from us to switch to narwhals. i guess linking to pull requests from other projects that switched to narwhals would be useful as well. discussion of the risks involved here are a few things off the top of my head, it would be nice to have these aspects detailed as well in the cost-benefit analysis: - narwhals stops being maintained and we end up having to maintain a fork. i guess unlikely in the short-term but you know it's hard to make predictions, especially about the future . - intersection with array api? narwhals can support dask for example, and with array-api-extra array api also supports dask, how does it interact together in our code base, any caveats there? - any consideration about the lazy aspects, any insights about supporting it, is it out of scope for now?"]

Name: 35_sort_hash_sorting_types
Representative_Docs: ["about sorting: i'm not surprised something like that happened due to the variability in the geometry of the problem, esp. this point i mentioned above: distribution of the data (small high number of unique values, repeated values, etc.): this affects a lot the benefits or not of different sorting strategies (or not sorting). the sort that is currently implemented in trees is really efficient for some cases (e.g. geometric law, binary variable, ...), even more than the simd-accelerated sort from numpy. and those cases are common for tabular data. numpy does a 2-way partitioning quicksort, while trees uses a 3-way partitioning quicksort. that perf regression after changing the sort", "then choosing the 'lowest' most frequent value this is where we need to be careful. if possible we'd like to not change the existing behavior on comparable dtypes., for instance but ordering on hash values has no guarantee to be the same. i agree with tim that the proposed hash solution looks good as long as it's deterministic. we still need to rely on the original values (not the hashes) in the cases that currently work to not change existing behavior. the solution presented in seems to satisfy all these requirements. that is why i like exploring the idea of detecting the problem and telling the user to use a tool like ordinalencoder on their data that might be tricky, or at least costly, since allows mixed typed input. so we'd need to loop through the whole dataset to detect that a column has mixed types. also, it's on purpose that the is more permissive than other estimators regarding its input because it's usually the first in line. so i'm not sure that allows the wide range of inputs that handles, need to check that.", "i see the current code is indeed not guarded against degenerate cases with a o(n) recursion depth (and thus o(n 2) complexity). quick recall about quick sort variants: - 2-way partition: partition the array in which is what is currently implemented, or which is what numpy does - 3-way partition: partition the array in . this is very efficient when there are a lot of duplicate values, but otherwise it's less efficient than the 2-way scheme. i think that all those schemes can degenerate in o(n 2) complexity for adversarial inputs, that's why we usually guard them with a heap sort. this mix is called intro sort. for instance, decision trees implement an intro sort with a 3-way partition quick sort. but for the current partitioning scheme used in ( ) the adversarial input is as simple as a constant array (or arrays with many duplicates), which is a very common case. so for now, i propose to simply change this to the numpy-style partitioning: which doesn't suffer from this problem. and on the long term, i think it would be nice to share the sorting code with decision trees to have one single file with: - the heap sort (not used directly, used by other sorts as a fallback) - a 2-way intro sort: 2-way quick sort with fallback to heap sort when recursion depth degenerates - a 3-way intro sort: 3-way quick sort with fallback to heap sort when recursion depth degenerates"]

Name: 37_callbacks_callback_meta_estimator
Representative_Docs: ["then we can investigate whether a second step where we don't impose that estimators within a meta estimator (or function that behaves like a meta-estimator) have their callbackcontext tree merged with the one of the parent estimator. in such situations, the callbacks may not provide their full capabilities. for instance progress bars could not be displayed in a single rich live object with bars inserted dynamically and intuitive layout, but would pop one below the other. after some considerations, this part seems quite far from the current design so pretty unrealistic for a sklearn 1.9 target. let's focus on the first part for the next release, that is make sure that a wrong usage of callbacks don't break user code.", "callbacks should can we avoid creating the context tree when no callbacks ? for meta-estimators, the answer is a definitive no. they can have subestimators with callbacks which need the full context tree to work optimally. for regular estimators, it could make sense. the performance for creating contexts is not prohibitive: 1-2us. this is small for one iteration even for quick estimators and small datasets (knowing that callbacks don't go deeper than iterations involving the full dataset). but there might be cases where it matters. i would avoid doing it if it adds too much verbosity within every estimator, like adding conditional branches everywhere we create a subcontext or call callback hooks. but if we can enable the feature without changing the existing callback support api implementation, why not.", "callbacks fea define behavior for callbacks registered on estimators used in third party functions from and related to the current implementation requires that estimators used in meta-estimators can only have callbacks if the meta-estimator supports callbacks. it may be a too strong requirement, or at least we need to define it properly, for user code. a typical problematic use case is when several clones of an estimator are fitted in parallel in a user defined function (like a custom cross validation). it implies in particular that we settle on the expected behavior of clone w.r.t callbacks. an easy first step would be that clone discards the callbacks. afterall callbacks are not constructor parameters strictly. with that callbacks would just be disabled for estimators used in non-callback-aware code. (it would be silent though) then we can investigate whether a second step where we don't impose that estimators within a meta estimator (or function that behaves like a meta-estimator) have their callbackcontext tree merged with the one of the parent estimator. in such situations, the callbacks may not provide their full capabilities. for instance progress bars could not be displayed in a single rich live object with bars inserted dynamically and intuitive layout, but would pop one below the other. ping"]

Name: 38_xgboost_subsampling_xgboost lightgbm_notebook
Representative_Docs: ['heyo full notebook here: on your question about xgboost defaults: returns for parameters such as and . these defaults resolve inside the c layer ( ) and are not accessible from the python api. i ve noted the relevant parameters in cell 5 of the notebook. main findings: (not implemented in sklearn hgbt) is significantly over-represented in pareto-optimal xgboost configurations (mann-whitney p 0.001, consistent across classification and regression datasets). restricting xgboost runs to reduces its advantage over histgradientboostingtree substantially, suggesting column subsampling may be a major contributor to the observed performance gap. (available in gradientboostingtree but not in histgradientboostingtree) shows a similar pattern, which aligns with the motivation behind 16062. and show weaker and less consistent signals in the pareto analysis, suggesting lower practical priority. happy to open a pr with a small standalone script (and a smoke test) if that would be more useful than the notebook. would you prefer that format?', "( this is not a reproducer, so i can't really answer. but xgboost doesn't have any issues if you use latest xgboost and scikit-learn versions. yes, it is all fixed with latest versions. from either i had to downgrade to sklearn 1.5.2 or upgrade xgboost to 3.0.", "this is not a reproducer, so i can't really answer. but xgboost doesn't have any issues if you use latest xgboost and scikit-learn versions."]

Name: 41_baseline_curve_non informative_roc
Representative_Docs: ["hi , thank you for collecting this here! i did some further reading in the meantime (and fixed at least one misunderstanding of mine), so let me try to summarize the issue(s). - my dismissal of the horizontal line derived from the always-positive classifier was wrong. one can construct it this way: have a model return completely random scores, and then tune the decision threshold between 0 and 1. this yields exactly that horizontal line. so: the horizontal line is valid ( associated with actual baseline models one can easily construct at every point along the line), and it is actually based on a 'chance' baseline. (might be worth pointing this out somewhere in the documentation, if this is not already the case?) - separately from this, flach and kull argue that in terms of the area under the pr curve, the baseline to beat is a curve that has f1 score equaling that of the always-positive classifier at every point along the curve. the interpretation is simple: if your model is below this line, you perform worse than a trivial always-positive classifier in terms of f1 . this is where the hyperbolic baseline comes from. i am actually not sure what the equivalent curve in the roc case would be, or whether it differs from the standard diagonal? note that at least to my current understanding, this baseline is 'virtual' in the sense that it is not trivially possible to actually construct models with the precision recall values specified by every point along the curve. - to complicate matters further, there is actually a third 'baseline-like' line that one could show: this is the unreachable region in pr space, described . points below this line cannot be reached by any classifier (with important consequences for the validity of naive aucpr comparisons, as spelled out both in this paper and the flach kull paper).", "for the roc curve, what we currently call chance level (the diagonal roc curve) is any non-informative baseline predictor: a predictor whose predictions do not depend on x. they can be any constant predict_proba predictions: 0, 1, the fraction of the majority class in the training set or any arbitrary constant prediction. or even a classifier that outputs a random predicted probability at each test point would also have a roc curve lying on the diagonal in the limit of a large prediction set. personally, i don't like the chance-level naming because it is a bit fuzzy, and it's not intuitive how a classifier that constantly predicts 1 or 0 can be related to chance . i would rather name this non-informative predictor or non-informative baseline (or event constant predictor ).", "i've removed the good first issue tag as judging by the 4 prs which all address this issue in a different way, i don't think we have reached consensus on how best to address this issue. i think we agree that 'chance' is not an ideal term. has suggested baseline while has suggested non-informative predictor or non-informative baseline (or event constant predictor ). i think it would be nice if the roc curve and pr curve chance baseline non-informative ... lines both had names that were somewhat consistent. let's get consensus on what to change it to before we start. -pet ( do you think we should re-think which 'baseline' line we include in our pr curve? or if we should include more than one? or that we should mention the others in documentation?"]

Name: 42_ppc64le_ci_github_runners
Representative_Docs: ['thanks for the clarification. just to confirm are you suggesting that we maintain ppc64le support through our fork, or were you envisioning something else? we ll start by making the necessary fixes in our fork and then open a pr to your repository with the ci ppc64le tag, so the ci runs only when needed. also, a quick question do you plan to upload the ppc64le wheels to pypi eventually, since that s our intended end goal? lastly, could you please open an issue in the actions-pz repository to install our github app for ci integration similar to this one:', 'ci: enable github actions app for ppc64le (power architecture) support hi scikit-learn team, we re reaching out to propose enabling ci support for the ppc64le (ibm power) architecture in your repository, as part of a broader effort to ensure cross-platform compatibility in the scientific python ecosystem. we re using a github actions (gha)-based runner service provided and maintained by ibm to run jobs for the ppc64le architecture. this setup has already been successfully integrated into projects like: cryptography ( tracking issue in numpy ( we d now like to propose enabling the github actions app in this repository to allow running ci jobs for ppc64le directly via github actions. this would support upstream compatibility and help ensure continued support for the power architecture in scikit-learn. key benefits: ephemeral and secure runners, isolated per job maintained by ibm, requires no setup effort from your side integrates with existing github actions workflows technical documentation and usage details: we re happy to assist with the setup or provide any additional details the team may need. thanks so much!', "support for ppc64le in scikit-learn ci proposed new feature or change: hi team, we would like to upstream support for the power (ppc64le) architecture in scikit-learn by adding a new ci job in wheels.yml. this will enable continuous integration (ci) support for ppc64le using a github actions self-hosted runner. what we've done so far 1. forking and building: - we forked the scikit-learn repository and successfully built and tested wheels for ppc64le using an ephemeral self-hosted runner on an osu power machine. 2. using cibuildwheel: - we leveraged cibuildwheel, the same tool used for building wheels on other architectures and confirmed that it works smoothly for ppc64le. 3. modifications: - the key change involves adding a new job for ppc64le in the wheels.yml file. - this ensures that scikit-learn can be built and tested automatically for power architecture alongside other existing platforms. why we're proposing these changes - enabling ci support for ppc64le ensures continuous testing and validation, improving compatibility and reliability for power users. - the self-hosted runner ensures minimal impact on existing ci cd pipelines since it only runs when triggered. - other projects, such as pytorch, have adopted a similar approach for alternative architectures like s390x details of self-hosted runner setup - we have successfully set up an ephemeral self-hosted runner for ppc64le on an osu vm, following an approach similar to s390x. ( - the runner remains in listening mode and can be triggered by specific workflows (e.g., trunk updates), ensuring efficient usage. details of osu and ibm power support through a partnership between ibm and the oregon state university (osu) open source lab (osl), infrastructure is provided for open-source development: - architecture: power little-endian (le) - virtualization: kernel-based virtual machine (kvm) - access: secure shell (ssh) and openstack api gui - capabilities: supports jenkins and integration into external ci cd pipelines alternative approach: qemu-based ci - we also tested a qemu-based approach, updating wheels.yml accordingly. - using a qemu ubuntu docker setup on a github runner, we successfully built the wheels. - however, the qemu-based build took approximately 6 hours, whereas the self-hosted runner completes the build in just 22 minutes, making it a significantly more efficient solution. request for feedback we would appreciate your feedback on: 1. feasibility and design of the proposed changes. 2. any modifications or requirements needed for upstreaming ppc64le support. we are happy to provide further details or assistance as needed. looking forward to your thoughts!"]
